desc: The Chapter Containing the Section on Ethics (Sīlakkhandhavagga) is a chapter of 13 discourses. Each of these contains a long passage on the Gradual Training in ethics, meditation, and wisdom. The chapter is named after the first of these sections. The two other known versions of the Dīrghāgama (in Chinese and Sanskrit) also contain a similar chapter. Despite the monastic nature of the central teaching, most of these discourses are presented in dialog with lay people, with a strong emphasis on the relation between the Buddha’s teachings and other contemporary movements.

This chapter contains a diverse range of discourses. Several focus on the events surrounding the Buddha’s death, while others range into fabulous scenarios set among the gods, and still others are grounded in detailed discussions of doctrine.

Like the previous chapter, this contains a diverse range of discourses. It is named after the first discourse in the chapter. Among the discourses here are legendary accounts of the history and future of our world, which are extremely famous and influential in Buddhist circles.

While others may praise or criticize the Buddha, they tend to focus on trivial details. The Buddha presents an analysis of 62 kinds of wrong view, seeing through which one becomes detached from meaningless speculations.

The newly crowned King Ajātasattu is disturbed by the violent means by which he achieved the crown. He visits the Buddha to find peace of mind, and asks him about the benefits of spiritual practice. This is one of the greatest literary and spiritual texts of early Buddhism.
A young brahmin student attacks the Buddha’s family, but is put in his place.
A reputed brahmin visits the Buddha, despite the reservations of other brahmins. They discuss the true meaning of a brahmin, and the Buddha skillfully draws him around to his own point of view.

A brahmin wishes to undertake a great sacrifice, and asks for the Buddha’s advice. The Buddha tells a legend of the past, in which a king is persuaded to give up violent sacrifice, and instead to devote his resources to supporting the needy citizens of his realm. However, even such a beneficial and non-violent sacrifice pales in comparison to the spiritual sacrifice of giving up attachments.
The Buddha explains to a diverse group of lay people how the results of meditation depend on the manner of development.
This discourse is mostly quoted by the Buddha in the previous.

The Buddha is challenged by a naked ascetic on the topic of spiritual austerities. He points out that it is quite possible to perform all kinds of austere practices without having any inner purity of mind.
The Buddha discusses with a wanderer the nature of perception and how it evolves through deeper states of meditation. None of these, however, should be identified with a self or soul.
Shortly after the Buddha’s death, Venerable Ānanda is invited to explain the core teachings.
The Buddha refuses to perform miracles, explaining that this is not the right way to inspire faith. He goes on to tell the story of a monk whose misguided quest for answers led him as far as Brahmā.
A brahmin has fallen into the idea that there is no point in trying to offer spiritual help to others. The Buddha goes to see him, and persuades him of the genuine benefits of spiritual teaching.
A number of brahmins are discussing the true path to Brahmā. Contesting the claims to authority based on the Vedas, the Buddha insists that only personal experience can lead to the truth.
The Buddha teaches about the six Buddhas of the past, and tells a lengthy account of one of those, Vipassī.

Rejecting Venerable Ānanda’s claim to easily understand dependent origination, the Buddha presents a complex and demanding analysis, revealing hidden nuances and implications of this central teaching.

The longest of all discourses, this extended narrative tells of the events surrounding the Buddha’s death. Full of vivid and moving details, it is an ideal entry point into knowing the Buddha as a person, and understanding how the Buddhist community coped with his passing.
An elaborate story of a past life of the Buddha as a legendary king who renounced all to practice meditation.
Beginning with an account of the fates of disciples who had recently passed away, the scene shifts to a discussion of Dhamma held by the gods.
A minor deity informs the Buddha of the conversations and business of the gods.

When deities from all realms gather in homage to the Buddha, he gives a series of verses describing them. These verses, which are commonly chanted in Theravadin countries, give one of the most detailed descriptions of the deities worshiped at the the time of the Buddha.
After hearing a love song from a god of music, the Buddha engages in a deep discussion with Sakka on the conditioned origin of attachment and suffering.

The Buddha details the seventh factor of the noble eightfold path, mindfulness meditation. This discourse is essentially identical to MN 10, with the addition of an extended section on the four noble truths derived from MN 141.

This is a long and entertaining debate between a monk and a skeptic, who went to elaborate and bizarre lengths to prove that there is no such thing as an afterlife. The discourse contains a colorful series of parables and examples.
When Sunakkhatta threatens to disrobe, the Buddha is unimpressed. Rejecting showy displays of asceticism or wondrous powers, he demonstrates his pre-eminence.

This discourse gives a specially good example of dialog between religions. The Buddha insists that he is not interested to make anyone give up their teacher or practices, but only to help people let go of suffering.

In illustration of his dictum that one should rely on oneself, the Buddha gives a detailed account of the fall of a kingly lineage of the past, and the subsequent degeneration of society. This process, however, is not over, as the Buddha predicts that eventually society will fall into utter chaos. But far in the future, another Buddha, Metteyya, will arise in a time of peace and plenty.

In contrast with the brahmin’s self-serving mythologies of the past, the Buddha presents an account of evolution that shows how human choices are an integral part of the ecological balance, and how excessive greed destroys the order of nature.
Shortly before he passes away, Venerable Sāriputta visits the Buddha and utters a moving eulogy of his great teacher.

Following the death of Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, the leader of the Jains, the Buddha emphasizes the stability and maturity of his own community. He encourages the community to come together after his death and recite the teachings in harmony.
This presents the brahmanical prophecy of the Great Man, and explains the 32 marks in detail. This discourse contains some of the latest and most complex verse forms in the canon.

The Buddha encounters a young man who honors his dead parents by performing rituals. The Buddha recasts the meaningless rites in terms of virtuous conduct. This is the most detailed discourse on ethics for lay people.
Mighty spirits hold a congregation, and warn the Buddha that, since not all spirits are friendly, the mendicants should learn verses of protection.
The Buddha encourages Venerable Sāriputta to teach the mendicants, and he offers an extended listing of Buddhist doctrines arranged in numerical sequence.
This is similar to the previous, but with a different manner of exposition. These two discourses anticipate some of the methods of the Abhidhamma.
This chapter, though beginning with the abstruse Mūlapariyāya Sutta, mostly contains foundational teachings and can, as a whole, serve as an introduction to the discourses.
Beginning with two discourses containing a “lion’s roar”, this chapter deals with suffering, community life, and practical meditation advice.
A diverse chapter including biography, non-violence, not-self, and an influential teaching on the progress of meditation.
Discourses arranged as pairs of longer and shorter texts.
A similar arrangement to the previous.
This chapter is addressed to a diverse range of lay people.
Ten discourses to monks, many of them focusing on matters of discipline.
The Buddha in dialog with ascetics and wanderers.
Various dialogs with kings and princes, many of whom followed the Buddha.
The Buddha engages with the powerful caste of brahmins, contesting their claims to spiritual authority.
Diverse teachings.
Many of the discourses in this chapter delve into complex and analytical presentations of core teachings. It includes important discourses on meditation.
Named after the first discourses, which deal with emptiness, this chapter presents less analytical and more narrative texts.
A series of discourses presented as technical analyses of shorter teachings.
Most discourses in this chapter deal with the six sense fields.

The Buddha examines how the notion of a permanent self emerges from the process of perception. A wide range of phenomena are considered, embracing both naturalistic and cosmological dimensions. An unawakened person interprets experience in terms of a self, while those more advanced have the same experiences without attachment.

The diverse problems of the spiritual journey demand a diverse range of responses. Rather than applying the same solution to every problem, the Buddha outlines seven methods of dealing with defilements, each of which works in certain cases.

Some of the Buddha’s students inherit from him only material profits and fame. But his true inheritance is the spiritual path, the way of contentment. Venerable Sāriputta explains how by following the Buddha’s example we can experience the fruits of the path.

The Buddha explains the difficulties of living in the wilderness, and how they are overcome by purity of conduct and meditation. He recounts some of the fears and obstacles he faced during his own practice.

The Buddha’s chief disciples, Sāriputta and Moggallāna, use a simile of a tarnished bowl to illustrate the blemishes of the mind and conduct. They emphasize how the crucial thing is not so much whether there are blemishes, but whether we are aware of them.
According to the Buddha, careful observance of ethical precepts is the foundation of all higher achievements in the spiritual life.

The many different kinds of impurities that defile the mind are compared to a dirty cloth. When the mind is clean we find joy, which leads to states of higher consciousness. Finally, the Buddha rejects the Brahmanical notion that purity comes from bathing in sacred rivers.

The Buddha differentiates between peaceful meditation and spiritual practices that encompass the whole of life. He lists forty-four aspects, which he explains as “effacement”, the wearing away of conceit.

Venerable Sāriputta gives a detailed explanation of right view, the first factor of the noble eightfold path. At the prompting of the other mendicants, he approaches the topic from a wide range of perspectives.

Here the Buddha details the seventh factor of the noble eightfold path, mindfulness meditation. This collects many of the meditation teachings found throughout the canon, especially the foundational practices focusing on the body, and is regarded as one of the most important meditation discourses.

The Buddha declares that only those following his path can genuinely experience the four stages of awakening. This is because, while much is shared with other systems, none of them go so far as to fully reject all attachment to the idea of a self.

A disrobed monk, Sunakkhatta, attacks the Buddha’s teaching because it merely leads to the end of suffering. The Buddha counters that this is, in fact, praise, and goes on to enumerate his many profound and powerful achievements.

Challenged to show the difference between his teaching and that of other ascetics, the Buddha points out that they speak of letting go, but do not really understand why. He then explains in great detail the suffering that arises from attachment to sensual stimulation.

A lay person is puzzled at how, despite their long practice, they still have greedy or hateful thoughts. The Buddha explains the importance of absorption meditation for letting go such attachments. But he also criticizes self-mortification, and recounts a previous dialog with Jain ascetics.

Venerable Moggallāna raises the topic of admonishment, without which healthy community is not possible. He lists a number of qualities that will encourage others to think it worthwhile to admonish you in a constructive way.
The Buddha explains various ways one can become emotionally cut off from one’s spiritual community.

While living in the wilderness is great, not everyone is ready for it. The Buddha encourages meditators to reflect on whether one’s environment is genuinely supporting their meditation practice, and if not, to leave.

Challenged by a brahmin, the Buddha gives an enigmatic response on how conflict arises due to proliferation based on perceptions. Venerable Kaccāna draws out the detailed implications of this in one of the most insightful passages in the entire canon.
Recounting his own experiences in developing meditation, the Buddha explains how to understand harmful and harmless thoughts, and how to go beyond thought altogether.
In a practical meditation teaching, the Buddha describes five different approaches to stopping thoughts.

A discourse full of vibrant and memorable similes, on the importance of patience and love even when faced with abuse and criticism. The Buddha finishes with the simile of the saw, one of the most memorable similes found in the discourses.

One of the monks denies that prohibited conduct is really a problem. The monks and then the Buddha subject him to an impressive dressing down. The Buddha compares someone who understands only the letter of the teachings to someone who grabs a snake by the tail, and also invokes the famous simile of the raft.
In a curious discourse laden with evocative imagery, a deity presents a riddle to a mendicant, who seeks an answer from the Buddha.
Venerable Sāriputta seeks a dialog with an esteemed monk, Venerable Puṇṇa Mantāniputta, and they discuss the stages of purification.
The Buddha compares getting trapped by Māra with a deer getting caught in a snare, illustrating the ever more complex strategies employed by hunter and hunted.

This is one of the most important biographical discourses, telling the Buddha’s experiences from leaving home to realizing awakening. Throughout, he was driven by the imperative to fully escape from rebirth and suffering.

The Buddha cautions against swift conclusions about a teacher’s spiritual accomplishments, comparing it to the care a tracker would use when tracking elephants. He presents the full training of a monastic.

Sāriputta gives an elaborate demonstration of how, just as any footprint can fit inside an elephant’s, all the Buddha’s teaching can fit inside the four noble truths. This offers an overall template for organizing the Buddha’s teachings.

Following the incident with Devadatta, the Buddha cautions the mendicants against becoming complacent with superficial benefits of spiritual life and points to liberation as the true heart of the teaching.
Similar to the previous. After the incident with Devadatta, the Buddha cautions the mendicants against becoming complacent and points to liberation as the true heart of the teaching.

The Buddha comes across three mendicants practicing diligently and harmoniously, and asks them how they do it. Reluctant to disclose their higher attainments, they explain how they deal with the practical affairs of living together. But when pressed by the Buddha, they reveal their meditation attainments.

Several senior mendicants, reveling in the beauty of the night, discuss what kind of practitioner would adorn the park. They take their answers to the Buddha, who praises their answers, but gives his own twist.
For eleven reasons a cowherd is not able to properly look after a herd. The Buddha compares this to the spiritual growth of a mendicant.
Drawing parallels with a cowherd guiding his herd across a dangerous river, the Buddha presents the various kinds of enlightened disciples who cross the stream of transmigration.
Saccaka was a debater, who challenged the Buddha to a contest. Despite his bragging, the Buddha is not at all perturbed at his attacks.

In a less confrontational meeting, the Buddha and Saccaka discuss the difference between physical and mental development. The Buddha gives a long account of the various practices he did before awakening, detailing the astonishing lengths he took to mortify the body.
Moggallāna visits the heaven of Sakka, the lord of gods, to see whether he really understands what the Buddha is teaching.

To counter the wrong view that a self-identical consciousness transmigrates from one life to the next, the Buddha teaches dependent origination, showing that consciousness invariably arises dependent on conditions.
The Buddha encourages the mendicants to live up to their name, by actually practicing in a way that meets or exceeds the expectations people have for renunciants.
The labels of being a spiritual practitioner don’t just come from external trappings, but from sincere inner change.

The Buddha explains to a group of brahmins the conduct leading to rebirth in higher or lower states, including detailed explanations of the ten core practices which lay people should undertake, and which also form the basis for liberation.
Similar to the previous. The Buddha explains the conduct leading to rebirth in higher or lower states, including detailed explanations of the ten core practices.
A series of questions and answers between Sāriputta and Mahākoṭṭhita, examining various subtle and abstruse aspects of the teachings.
The layman Visākha asks the nun Dhammadinnā about various difficult matters, including some of the highest meditation attainments. The Buddha fully endorses her answers.
The Buddha explains how taking up different practices may have harmful or beneficial results. The memorable simile of the creeper shows how insidious temptations can be.
While we all want to be happy, we often find the opposite happens. The Buddha explains why.
While some spiritual teachers prefer to remain in obscurity, the Buddha not only encouraged his followers to closely investigate him, but gave them a detailed and demanding method to do so.

Despite the Buddha’s presence, the monks of Kosambi fell into a deep and bitter dispute. The Buddha taught the reluctant monks to develop love and harmony, reminding them of the state of peace that they sought.
The Buddha ascends to a high heavenly realm where he engages in a cosmic contest with a powerful divinity, who had fallen into the delusion that he was eternal and all-powerful.
Māra, the trickster and god of death, tried to annoy Moggallāna. He not only failed but was subject to a stern sermon warning of the dangers of attacking the Buddha’s disciples.

The Buddha discusses mindfulness meditation with lay practitioners. Contrasting the openness of animals with the duplicity of humans, he explains how to practice in a way that causes no harm to oneself or others.
Asked by a householder to teach a path to freedom, Venerable Ānanda explains no less than eleven meditative states that may serve as doors to the deathless.

The Buddha is invited by his family, the Sakyans of Kapilavatthu, to inaugurate a new community hall. He invites Venerable Ānanda to explain in detail the stages of spiritual practice for a lay trainee.
When Potaliya got upset at being referred to as “householder”, the Buddha quizzed him as to the true nature of attachment and renunciation.
The Buddha’s personal doctor, Jīvaka, hears criticisms of the Buddha’s policy regarding eating meat, and asks him about it.

The Buddha disagrees with a Jain ascetic on the question of whether physical or mental deeds are more important. When he hears of this, the Jain disciple Upāli decides to visit the Buddha and refute him, and proceeds despite all warnings.

Some ascetics in ancient India undertook extreme practices, such as a vow to behave like an ox or a dog. The Buddha meets two such individuals, and is reluctantly pressed to reveal the kammic outcomes of such practice.
The leader of the Jains, Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, gives his disciple Prince Abhaya a dilemma to pose to the Buddha, supposing that this will show his weakness. Things don’t go quite as planned.

The Buddha resolves a disagreement on the number of kinds of feelings that he taught, pointing out that different ways of teaching are appropriate in different contexts, and should not be a cause of disputes. He goes on to show the importance of pleasure in developing higher meditation.

The Buddha teaches a group of uncommitted householders how to use a rational reflection to arrive at practices and principles that are guaranteed to have a good outcome, even if we don’t know all the variables.
Using the “object lesson” of a cup of water, the Buddha explains to his son, Rāhula, the importance of telling the truth and reflecting on one’s motives.

The Buddha tells Rāhula to meditate on not-self, which he immediately puts into practice. Seeing him, Venerable Sāriputta advises him to develop breath meditation, but the Buddha suggests a wide range of different practices first.

A monk demands that the Buddha answer his metaphysical questions, or else he will disrobe. The Buddha compares him to a man struck by an arrow, who refuses treatment until he can have all his questions about the arrow and the archer answered.
A little baby has no wrong views or intentions, but the underlying tendency for these things is still there. Without practicing, they will inevitably recur.
A monk refuses to follow the rule forbidding eating after noon, but is filled with remorse and forgiven.

Again raising the rule regarding eating, but this time as a reflection of gratitude for the Buddha in eliminating things that cause complexity and stress. The Buddha emphasizes how attachment even to little things can be dangerous.
After dismissing some unruly monks, the Buddha is persuaded to relent, and teaches them four dangers for those gone forth.
Those who practice do so not because they are failures, but because they aspire to higher freedom. When he speaks of the attainments of disciples, the Buddha does so in order to inspire.
A monk comes down to the community from the wilderness, but doesn’t behave properly. Venerable Sāriputta explains how a mendicant should behave, whether in forest or town.
A third discourse that presents the health benefits of eating in one part of the day, and the reluctance of some mendicants to follow this.
The Buddha denies being omniscient, and sets forth the three higher knowledges that form the core of his awakened insight.
Refusing to take a stance regarding useless metaphysical speculations, the Buddha illustrates the spiritual goal with the simile of a flame going out.
In the final installment of the “Vacchagotta trilogy”, Vacchagotta lets go his obsession with meaningless speculation, and asks about practice.

Deftly outmaneuvering an extreme skeptic, the Buddha discusses the outcomes of belief and disbelief. Rather than getting stuck in abstractions, he encourages staying close to the feelings one experiences.

Accused by a hedonist of being too negative, the Buddha recounts the luxury of his upbringing, and his realization of how little value there was in such things. Through renunciation he found a far greater pleasure.
Venerable Ānanda teaches a group of wanderers how there are many different approaches to the spiritual life, many of which lead nowhere.
Unlike many teachers, the Buddha’s followers treat him with genuine love and respect, since they see the sincerity of his teaching and practice.
A wanderer teaches that a person has reached the highest attainment when they keep four basic ethical precepts. The Buddha’s standards are considerably higher.

A wanderer teaches his doctrine of the “highest splendor” but is unable to give a satisfactory account of what that means. The Buddha memorably compares him to someone who is in love with an idealized women who he has never met.

Starting off similar to the previous, the Buddha goes on to explain that one is not converted to his teaching just because of clever arguments, but because you see in yourself the results of the practice.

The Buddha relates an unusual account of a past life in the time of the previous Buddha, Kassapa. At that time he was not interested in Dhamma, and had to be forced to go see the Buddha. This discourse is important in understanding the development of the Bodhisattva doctrine.

A wealthy young man, Raṭṭhapāla, has a strong aspiration to go forth, but has to prevail against the reluctance of his parents. Even after he became a monk, his parents tried to persuade him to disrobe. The discourse ends with a moving series of teachings on the fragility of the world.
A rare extended mythic narrative, telling of an ancient kingly lineage and their eventual downfall.
In Madhurā, towards the north-eastern limit of the Buddha’s reach during his life, King Avantiputta asks Venerable Mahākaccāna regarding the brahmanical claim to be the highest caste.
Admitting that he used to believe that pleasure was to be gained through pain, the Buddha explains how his practice showed him the fallacy of that idea.

Ignoring warnings, the Buddha ventures into the domain of the notorious killer Aṅgulimāla and succeeds in converting him to the path of non-violence. After becoming a monk Aṅgulimāla still suffered for his past deeds, but only to a small extent. He uses his new commitment to non-violence to help a woman in labor.

A rare glimpse into the marital life of King Pasenadi, and how he is led to the Dhamma by his Queen, the incomparable Mallikā. She confirms the Buddha’s teaching that our loved ones bring us sorrow; but that’s not something a husband, father, and king wants to hear.
King Pasenadi takes a chance to visit Venerable Ānanda, where he asks about skillful and unskillful behavior, and what is praised by the Buddha. He offers Ānanda a valuable cloth in gratitude.
King Pasenadi, near the end of his life, visits the Buddha, and shows moving devotion and love for his teacher.
King Pasenadi questions the Buddha on miscellaneous matters: caste, omniscience, and the gods among them.

The oldest and most respected brahmin of the age sends a student to examine the Buddha, and he spends several months following his every move before reporting back. Convinced that the Buddha fulfills an ancient prophecy of the Great Man, the brahmin becomes his disciple.

A brahmanical ascetic named Keṇiya invites the entire Saṅgha for a meal. When the brahmin Sela sees what is happening, he visits the Buddha and expresses his delight in a moving series of devotional verses.
A precocious brahmin student is encouraged against his wishes to challenge the Buddha on the question of caste. His reluctance turns out to be justified.
A brahmin denies that there is such a thing as a principled renunciate life, but Venerable Udena persuades him otherwise.

The reputed brahmin Caṅkī goes with a large group to visit the Buddha, despite the reservations of other brahmins. A precocious student challenges the Buddha, affirming the validity of the Vedic scriptures. The Buddha gives a detailed explanation of how true understanding gradually emerges through spiritual education.
A brahmin claims that one deserves service and privilege depending on caste, but the Buddha counters that it is conduct, not caste, that show a person’s worth.
A corrupt tax-collector is redeemed by his encounter with Venerable Sāriputta.

Two brahmin students ask the Buddha about what makes a brahmin: birth or deeds? the Buddha points out that, while the species of animals are determined by birth, for humans what matters is how you chose to live. This discourse anticipates the modern view that there are no such things as clearly defined racial differences among humans.
Working hard is not valuable in and of itself; what matters is the outcome. And just as in lay life, spiritual practice may or may not lead to fruitful results.
Angered by the devotion of a brahmin lady, a brahmin visits the Buddha. He positions himself against traditionalists and rationalists, as someone whose teaching is based on direct experience.

The Buddha tackles a group of Jain ascetics, pressing them on their claim to be practicing to end all suffering by self-mortification. He points out a series of fallacies in their logic, and explains his own middle way.
A middle length version of the more famous Brahmajala Sutta (DN1), this surveys a range of speculative views and dismisses them all.
The Buddha teaches the monks to not dispute about the fundamental teachings, but to always strive for harmony.

Hearing of the death of the Jain leader Nigaṇṭha Nātaputta, the Buddha encourages the Saṅgha to swiftly resolve any disputes. He lays down a series of seven methods for resolving disputes. These form the foundation for the monastic code.
Not all of those who claim to be awakened are genuine. The Buddha teaches how true spiritual progress depends on an irreversible letting go of the forces that lead to suffering.
Beginning with profound meditation absorption, the Buddha goes on to deeper and deeper levels, showing how insight on this basis leads to the detaching of consciousness from any form of rebirth.

The Buddha compares the training of an accountant with the step by step spiritual path of his followers. But even with such a well explained path, the Buddha can only show the way, and it is up to us to walk it.

Amid rising military tensions after the Buddha’s death, Venerable Ānanda is questioned about how the Saṅgha planned to continue in their teacher’s absence. As the Buddha refused to appoint a successor, the teaching and practice that he laid down become the teacher, and the Saṅgha resolves issues by consensus.

On a lovely full moon night, one of the mendicants presents the Buddha with a series of questions that go to the heart of the teaching. But when he hears of the doctrine of not-self, another mendicant is unable to grasp the meaning.
A good person is able to understand a bad person, but not vice versa.
The Buddha describes in technical detail the process of insight of Venerable Sāriputta. Many ideas and terms in this text anticipate the Abhidhamma.
If someone claims to be awakened, their claim should be interrogated with a detailed series of detailed questions. Only if they can answer them clearly should the claim be accepted.
The Buddha explains that a truly good person does not disparage others or feel superior because of their attainment.
The Buddha sets up a framework on things to be cultivated or avoided, and Venerable Sāriputta volunteers to elaborate.
Beginning by praising a wise person, the Buddha goes on to explain that one becomes wise by inquiring into the elements, sense fields, dependent origination, and what is possible and impossible.
Reflecting on the changes that even geographical features undergo, the Buddha then recounts the names of sages of the past who have lived in Mount Isigili near Rājagaha.
A discourse on the prerequisites of right samādhi that emphasizes the interrelationship and mutual support of all the factors of the eightfold path.
Surrounded by many well-practiced mendicants, the Buddha teaches mindfulness of breathing in detail, showing how it relates to the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
This focuses on the first aspect of mindfulness meditation, the observation of the body. This set of practices, simple as they seem, have far-reaching benefits.
The Buddha explains how one can make a wish to be reborn in different realms.
The Buddha describes his own practice of the meditation on emptiness.
A group of mendicants have taken to socializing too much, so the Buddha teaches on the importance of seclusion in order to enter fully into emptiness.

Venerable Ānanda is invited by the Buddha to speak on the Buddha’s amazing qualities, and proceeds to list a series of apparently miraculous events accompanying his birth. The Buddha caps it off by explaining what he thinks is really amazing about himself.

Venerable Bakkula, regarded as the healthiest of the mendicants, explains to an old friend his strict and austere practice. The unusual form of this discourse suggests it was added to the canon some time after the Buddha’s death.

A young monk is unable to persuade a prince of the blessings of peace of mind. The Buddha offers similes based on training an elephant that would have been successful, as this was a field the prince was familiar with.
Success in the spiritual life does not depend on any vows you may or may not make, but on whether you practice well.
A lay person becomes confused when encouraged to develop the “limitless” and “expansive” liberations, and asks Venerable Anuruddha to explain whether they are the same or different.

A second discourse set at the quarrel of Kosambi, this depicts the Buddha, having failed to achieve reconciliation between the disputing mendicants, leaving the monastery. He spends time in the wilderness before encountering an inspiring community of practicing monks. There he discusses in detail obstacles to meditation that he encountered before awakening.
A fool suffers both in this life and the next, while the astute benefits in both respects.
Expanding on the previous, this discourse contains the most detailed descriptions of the horrors of hell.
This discourse opens with a short but powerful set of verses extolling the benefits of insight into the here and now, followed by an explanation.
The same discourse as MN 131, but spoken by Venerable Ānanda.
The verses from MN 131 are explained in a different way by Venerable Mahakaccāna.
A monk who does not know the verses from MN 131 is encouraged by a deity to learn them.
The Buddha explains to a brahmin how your deeds in past lives affect you in this life.
Confronted with an overly simplistic version of his own teachings, the Buddha emphasizes the often overlooked nuances and qualifications in how karma plays out.
A detailed analysis of the six senses and the relation to emotional and cognitive processes.
The Buddha gives a brief and enigmatic statement on the ways consciousness may become attached. Venerable Mahākaccāna is invited by the mendicants to draw out the implications.
Achieving peace is no simple matter. The Buddha explains how to avoid conflict through contentment, right speech, understanding pleasure, and not insisting on local conventions.

While staying overnight in a potter’s workshop, the Buddha has a chance encounter with a monk who does not recognize him. They have a long and profound discussion based on the four elements. This is one of the most insightful and moving discourses in the canon.
Expanding on the Buddha’s first sermon, Venerable Sāriputta gives a detailed explanation of the four noble truths.

When his step-mother Mahāpajāpatī wishes to offer him a robe for his personal use, the Buddha encourages her to offer it to the entire Saṅgha instead. He goes on to explain that the best kind of offering to the Saṅgha is one given to the dual community of monks and nuns, headed by the Buddha.
As the great lay disciple Anāthapiṇḍika lies dying, Venerable Sāriputta visits him and gives a powerful teaching on non-attachment.
The monk Channa is suffering a painful terminal illness and wishes to take his own life.
On the eve of his departure to a distant country, full of wild and unpredictable people, Venerable Puṇṇa is asked by the Buddha how he would respond if attacked there.
When asked to teach the nuns, Venerable Nandaka proceeds by inviting them to engage with his discourse and ask if there is anything that needs further explanation.
The Buddha takes Rāhula with him to a secluded spot in order to lead him on to liberation.
The Buddha analyzes the six senses from six perspectives, and demonstrates the emptiness of all of them.
Explains how insight into the six senses is integrated with the eightfold path and leads to liberation.
In discussion with a group of householders, the Buddha helps them to distinguish those spiritual practitioners who are truly worthy of respect.

The Buddha notices Venerable Sāriputta’s glowing complexion, which is the result of his deep meditation. He then presents a series of reflections by which a mendicant can be sure that they are worthy of their alms-food.

Times that are supportive for meditation practice.
A mother and son, though ordained as monk and nun, have sexual relations. The Buddha launches a powerful attack on the dangers of sex.
A monk, dissatisfied with monastic life, seeks support from his mentor.
Topics that are worthy regularly reflecting on, whether as a lay person or renunciant.
When some wild young men show deep respect for the Buddha, a family elder is astonished.
Things that are hard to find in someone who has gone forth when old.
Things that are hard to find in someone who has gone forth when old.
Five meditations are very beneficial.
Five meditations lead to the deathless.
Noble growth for a male disciple.
Noble growth for a female disciple.
Qualifications for taking part in a discussion on Dharma.
Qualifications for sharing Dharma.
The four bases of psychic power, together with energy, lead to awakening.
The Buddha developed the bases for psychic power, which lead to his awakening.
Five meditations that lead to disillusionment with the world.
Five meditations that lead to the end of defilements.
Five meditations lead to freedom. The Buddha supplies five similes, which subtly illustrate five aspects of awakening.
Five meditations lead to freedom. The Buddha supplies five similes, which subtly illustrate five aspects of awakening.
The difference between someone who merely studies the letter of the Dharma, and someone who truly lives by it.
The difference between someone who merely studies the letter of the Dharma, and someone who truly lives by it.
Some warriors, like some monks, falter before the threat of battle, while others emerge victorious.
Some warriors, like some monks, are killed or injured in battle, while others emerge victorious.
The dangers of living in a wilderness among wild beasts and so on.
The uncertainties of life may prevent your practice.
When the teachers and leaders of the Saṅgha are not well practiced, their weakness will be passed down the generations.
The Saṅgha may forsake the simple life and indulge in luxuries.
Senior mendicants with strong defilements are disliked.
Senior mendicants with strong defilements are disliked.
Senior mendicants who are manipulative and opportunistic are disliked.
Qualities making a senior mendicant liked or disliked.
A senior mendicant with no endurance is disliked.
A senior mendicant is liked if they can analyze and explain the Dharma well, and if they are helpful in the monastery.
A senior mendicant with morality, learning, who is a good speaker and successful meditator is liked.
Even if a senior mendicant has many good qualities, they can still lead people astray if they have wrong view.
Even if a mendicant is a trainee, they won’t progress if they don’t focus on practice. In brief.
Even if a mendicant is a trainee, they won’t progress if they don’t focus on practice. In detail.
Five spiritual accomplishments culminating in wisdom.
Five spiritual accomplishments culminating in freedom.
When people are asked whether they are enlightened, they might answer for different reasons.
Living comfortably by practicing meditation.
Supported by five factors, including four aspects of textual analysis, one will soon realize the unshakable.
Supported by five factors, one who practices mindfulness of breathing will soon realize the unshakable.
Supported by five factors, one who practices mindfulness of breathing will soon realize the unshakable.
Supported by five factors, one who practices mindfulness of breathing will soon realize the unshakable.
A lion is always respectful when it kills. So too when the Buddha teaches he always respects his audience.
Kakudha, Venerable Moggallāna’s lay supporter, having recently passed away, returned to warn him about Devadatta’s fall. The Buddha analyses different kinds of teacher.
Five factors in which a trainee is assured.
Five reasons why even a good mendicant might be suspected of bad behavior.
Five tricks used by thieves to hide, and similar tricks used by bad mendicants.
Five qualities of the most refined type of ascetic.
Five ways in which a mendicant can live comfortably with their spiritual companions.
The Buddha explains to Ānanda five ways in which a mendicant can live comfortably with their spiritual companions.
Five factors of a worthy mendicant.
Five factors of a worthy mendicant.
Five factors by which a mendicant covers all the directions.
Five factors that show whether a mendicant is ready to live in the wilderness.
Five qualities that endear a mendicant who visits families.
Five factors to be observed by a companion who follows on alms-round; this would typically be a novice or junior mendicant.
To enter samādhi, a mendicant must be able to endure sense impacts.
Five qualities to instill in recently ordained mendicants.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five good and five bad qualities that determine a nun’s destiny after death.
Five meditations recommended for sick mendicants.
Five meditations that lead to perfection or non-return.
Five qualities that make a patient hard to nurse.
Five requirements that a carer or nurse should meet before looking after a patient.
Things that assist or impede longevity.
Things that assist or impede longevity.
Five factors that show whether a mendicant is ready to live apart from the Sangha.
Things that make an ascetic happy or unhappy.
Five deeds that condemn the perpetrator to hell.
Worldly or moral losses and their consequences.
Five qualities by which a wheel-turning monarch rules justly, and five corresponding qualities by which the Buddha rolls the Wheel of Dhamma.
Five qualities by which a wheel-turning monarch’s son rules justly, and five corresponding qualities by which Sāriputta keeps rolling the Wheel of Dhamma.
Even a wheel-turning monarch has a king, as does the Buddha.
A wheel-turning monarch always lives in his own realm, and so does a mendicant whose mind is freed.
A wheel-turning monarch’s son aspires to kingship, as a mendicant aspires to awakening.
A wheel-turning monarch’s son aspires to be a viceroy, as a mendicant aspires to awakening.
Five people who sleep little.
Even a clumsy royal elephant is considered to be a royal elephant, and so too even a clumsy mendicant is still a mendicant.
A royal elephant who can’t endure sense impacts is unfit, and so too a mendicant.
A royal elephant with five factors is fit, and so too a mendicant.
Five people susceptible to various flaws.
Those who violate moral precepts may regret it or may not.
The Licchavis discuss the greatest treasures in the world. The Buddha, criticizing them for their fixation on sensual pleasures, suggests spiritual treasures.
Five meditations that train a mendicant to shift their perception at will.
Reasons one is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Don’t befriend a mendicant with five qualities; look instead for the five opposites.
Good and bad ways of offering gifts.
The way a good person gives gifts and the outcome.
A mendicant who has found only temporary freedom may decline for five reasons.
A mendicant who has found only temporary freedom may decline for five reasons.
Qualities that prevent one who is listening to the teaching from understanding.
Qualities that prevent one who is listening to the teaching from understanding.
Qualities that prevent one who is listening to the teaching from understanding.
Things that lead to the decline or the prosperity of the Dhamma.
Things that lead to the decline or the prosperity of the Dhamma.
Things that lead to the decline or the prosperity of the Dhamma.
Certain topics are inappropriate to discuss with certain kinds of people.
Confidence or lack thereof in mendicants is due to their practice.
When told Venerable Udāyī is teaching, the Buddha defines five factors that a Dhamma teacher should establish in themselves.
Five things that are hard to dispel once they have arisen
Five reflections to help eliminate resentment, in brief.
Five reflections to help eliminate resentment, an explanation by Sāriputta..
Qualifications for a mendicant to hold a Dhamma discussion.
Qualifications for a mendicant to share their way of life.
Sāriputta explains five reasons for asking a question, and the reason he asks a question.

Udāyī refuses to believe Sāriputta’s description of the achievements of an accomplished meditator. The Buddha dismisses Udāyī’s foolishness, and outlines the reasons senior mendicants should be respected.
Factors that a mendicant should establish in themselves before accusing another.
Lack of ethics prevents the attaining of samādhi, wisdom, and freedom.
Ānanda asks Sāriputta to explain what it means to be quick-witted, but Sāriputta gets Ānanda to explain it himself.
Ānanda asks Bhaddaji about the best sense objects, and corrects his answer.
Confidence or lack thereof in layfolk is due to their precepts.
Confidence or lack thereof in layfolk is due to their precepts.
Reasons one is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Breaking of precepts is a threat and a danger.
An outcaste is not born, but they make themselves an outcaste by their behavior.
The Buddha encourages Anāthapiṇḍika to not rest short with generosity, but to practice meditation too.
Five kinds of trade that are wrong livelihood.
Kings do not punish people for keeping precepts.
Qualities possessing which a lay person may claim to be a stream-enterer.
The Buddha tells of the layman Gavesī in the time of Buddha Kassapa, and how he went forth with all his followers.
Different reasons why someone might dwell in the wilderness.
Different reasons why someone might wear rag robes.
Different reasons why someone might dwell at the root of a tree.
Different reasons why someone might dwell in a charnel ground.
Different reasons why someone might dwell in the open air.
Different reasons why someone might never lie down.
Different reasons why someone might sleep wherever a mat is laid down.
Different reasons why someone might eat once per day.
Different reasons why someone might refuse food offered late.
Different reasons why someone might eat only from their bowl.
Ancient traditions of the brahmins that are these days only seen among dogs.
The brahmin Doṇa accuses the Buddha of not respecting brahmins, so the Buddha responds by analyzing the different kinds of brahmin.
Saṅgārava the brahmin asks why memorized hymns are sometimes clear to the mind and sometimes not. The Buddha answers with the five hindrances.
The brahmins Kāraṇapālī and Piṅgiyānī extol the good qualities of the Buddha.
During a visit by the Licchavis, the brahmin Piṅgiyānī utters a verse in praise of the Buddha.
Before his awakening, the bodhisatta had five great dreams that foretold profound aspects of his awakening and dispensation.
Weather forecasters remain ignorant of these five obstacles to rain.
Five factors of good speech.
When a renunciate visits a family, they make merit on five bases.
Five kinds of escape, showing how different aspects of suffering are escaped by different practices.
Things that lead to the long-lasting of the Dhamma after the Buddha’s death.
Five benefits of listening to the teaching.
Factors that make a thoroughbred worthy of a king.
Five spiritual powers.
Five kinds of emotional barrenness that can affect mendicants.
Five kinds of emotional shackle that can affect mendicants.
The five health benefits of eating porridge.
The five health problems of not cleaning one’s teeth with chew sticks.
Why you shouldn’t recite Dhamma in an overly drawn-out singing style.
Why you shouldn’t fall asleep unmindfully.
Why a mendicant shouldn’t speak ill of their spiritual companions.
Why a mendicant shouldn’t start arguments.
Five drawbacks of failure in morality, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks for a person who talks a lot, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks of intolerance, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks of intolerance, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks of uninspiring conduct, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks of uninspiring conduct, and the corresponding benefits of the reverse.
Five drawbacks of sitting around a fire.

For five reasons, a mendicant should wander a reasonable amount, not aimlessly.
For five reasons, a mendicant should wander a reasonable amount, not aimlessly.
For five reasons, a mendicant should stay in a monastery a reasonable length of time, not excessively.
For five reasons, a mendicant should stay in a monastery a reasonable length of time, not excessively.
A mendicant who visits families risks the following five problems.
A mendicant who visits families risks the following five problems.
Five drawbacks of riches, and five benefits.
Five drawbacks for families who eat overly late, and five benefits of eating at a reasonable hour.
Five ways a woman is like a black snake.
Five ways a woman is like a black snake.
Reasons a resident mendicant is admirable, and the reverse.
Reasons a resident mendicant is admirable.
Reasons a resident mendicant beautifies a monastery.
Reasons a resident mendicant helps a monastery.
How a mendicant shows compassion to lay folk.
Reasons a resident mendicant is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Reasons a resident mendicant is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Reasons a resident mendicant is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Reasons a resident mendicant is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Reasons a resident mendicant is cast down to hell or raised to heaven.
Drawbacks of bad conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad bodily conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad verbal conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad mental conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad bodily conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad verbal conduct, and the reverse.
Drawbacks of bad mental conduct, and the reverse.
Why a person may have the same defects as a charnel ground.
Placing faith in an individual is dangerous for five reasons.
Qualifications for one who should give an ordination.
Qualifications for one who should give dependence.
Qualifications for one who should have a novice as attendant.
Material and spiritual stinginess.
Getting rid of the five kinds of stinginess.
Give up stinginess to enter the first absorption.
Give up stinginess to enter the absorptions and higher attainments.
Give up stinginess to enter the first absorption.
Give up stinginess to enter the absorptions and higher attainments.
Qualities of one who is appointed to assign meals.
Qualities of one who is appointed to assign lodgings, and other monastery responsibilities.
Why a monk is cast down to hell or raised up to heaven.
Why a nun, etc. is cast down to hell or raised up to heaven.
Why an ājīvaka ascetic is cast down to hell or raised up to heaven.
Why various ascetics are cast down to hell or raised up to heaven.
Develop insight into greed starting with ugliness.
Develop insight into greed starting with impermanence.
Develop insight into greed starting with impermanence.
Develop insight into greed through the faculties.
Develop insight into greed through the powers.
Develop complete undestanding, etc., of greed.
A mendicant is worthy of gifts because of equanimity for the six senses.
A mendicant is worthy of gifts because of the six higher knowledges.
A mendicant is worthy of gifts because of six faculties.
A mendicant is worthy of gifts because of six powers.
A steed is worthy of the king for six reasons, and a mendicant is worthy for six similar reasons.
A steed is worthy of the king for six reasons, and a mendicant is worthy for six similar reasons.
A steed is worthy of the king for six reasons, and a mendicant is worthy for six similar reasons.
Six things that are unsurpassable.
Six topics for recollection.
The Buddha teaches his relative Mahānāma that a noble disciple often practices the six recollections, which lead to joy and peace.
Six warm-hearted qualities practiced among the mendicants.
Six warm-hearted qualities practiced among the mendicants.
The Buddha’s teachings are effective, so we can’t claim to have practiced them fully if we’re still afflicted by the various forms of suffering.
A mendicant who lives well and doesn’t waste time will have a good death.
A mendicant who lives well and doesn’t waste time dies without regret.

A sick householder is consoled by his wife, who says there is no need for him to be worried about dying, as she is well able to support the family and practice the Dhamma. He recovers, and visits the Buddha to sing the praises of his kind and wise wife.
Neither a king nor a mendicant gets anything accomplished if they just sleep all the time.
Those whose livelihood is the killing of animals do not prosper.
Many of those who practice mindfulness of death don’t do so urgently enough. Death might come to us at any moment.
A method for recollecting one’s own death that leads to urgency, diligence, and joy.
Even the gods know that some monks are falling away from their practice.
In the past, future, and present, practicing six good qualities protects against decline.
Sensual pleasures are perilous.
A mendicant who has fully mastered meditation could even split apart the Himalayas.
The six recollections are a way to escape from greed.
Mahākaccāna teaches that the six recollections are a way to escape from greed and lead to a mind as vast as space.
The right time to visit an esteemed mendicant is when you need spiritual guidance and support.
Some senior mendicants discuss the right time to see an esteemed mendicant, and Mahākaccāna answers by quoting the Buddha from AN 6.27.
When the Buddha asks about the topics for recollection, a monk reveals his ignorance. Ānanda then gives an unusual list of five recollections, which the Buddha supplements with a sixth.
There are things in the world considered to be unsurpassed, but none of them compare with the Dhamma.
Things leading to decline or non-decline.
A god speaks on things leading to decline or non-decline.
The Buddha recounts the events of the previous discourse.
Mahāmoggallāna is visited by a Brahmā god who had formerly been a mendicant, and they discuss which of the gods are stream-enterers.
Six kinds of perception that lead to wisdom.
If a mendicant nurses six kinds of defilements it leads to bitterness and disputes in the Saṅgha.
Seeing the laywoman Veḷukaṇṭakī preparing a meal for the Saṅgha, the Buddha celebrated by saying that giving is of great benefit when the donor and the recipient each possess three qualities.
The Buddha rebuts a brahmin’s claim that there is no such thing as actions initiated by oneself.
Greed, hate, and delusion are the roots of deeds.
The Buddha explains to Venerable Kimbila the reasons why Buddhism lasts long after the Buddha’s demise.
Even a simple block of wood can be perceived in different ways by a mendicant with advanced meditation.
When visited by a noisy crowd, the Buddha speaks to his attendant Nāgita of his love of seclusion and solitude, and of his regard for forest mendicants.
When going for a bath, the Buddha encounters a giant royal elephant. But a spiritual giant is even more impressive.
In response to criticism, the Buddha explains that it is difficult to compare the spiritual development of different individuals, so we should not be judgmental.
Poor people suffer when they get in debt. Spiritual practitioners who are devoid of good qualities suffer in a similar way.

In a discourse evidently set some time after the Buddha’s passing, Venerable Mahācunda urges different groups of meditators, whether they emphasize serenity or insight, to respect and support each other.
The Buddha explains to the wanderer Moliyasīvaka how the teaching may be realized in the present life.
The Buddha explains a brahmin how the teaching may be realized in the present life.

Two monks separately come to the Buddha and speak of awakening in terms of letting go conceit. The Buddha affirms that they are, in fact, awakened, and that such indirect means are the proper way to make such declarations.
Just as a tree without leaves cannot grow, sense restraint is the foundation for spiritual growth.
Venerables Ānanda and Sāriputta discuss how a mendicants gets to further their learning and understanding.
The Buddha explains to the brahmin Jāṇussoṇī the ambition, quest, commitment, insistence, and ultimate goal of aristocrats, brahmins, householders, women, bandits, and ascetics.
With a series of vivid similes, the Buddha shows how diligence leads to benefits in both this life and the next.

The monk Dhammika annoys both the monks and the layfolk so badly that they banish him. He comes to the Buddha, who consoles him and raises his spirits with a story, while at the same time showing him the error of his ways.
When Venerable Soṇa thinks of disrobing, the Buddha comes and encourages him with the famous simile of the lute that is tuned neither too loose nor too tight.
The Buddha consoles Venerable Phagguṇa on his deathbed, and he dies at peace. The Buddha then explains to Ānanda the benefits of hearing the Dhamma at the right time.

Ānanda asks the Buddha about the six classes of people described by the rival ascetic Pūraṇa Kassapa. The Buddha rejects them, and proposes an alternate scheme, emphasizing the importance ones deeds in this life.
The Buddha explains diverse methods for overcoming diverse kinds of defilements.
The householder Dārukammika claims to give gifts to arahants. But the Buddha warns him that it’s hard to know who is really an arahant, and encourage him instead to make offerings to the Saṅgha.

The junior monk Citta Hatthisāriputta rudely interrupts his seniors, and is admonished by Mahākoṭṭhita. His friends speak in his defense, but Mahākoṭṭhita warns them with a long explanation of how hard it is to know another’s mind. Ultimately, Citta disrobes.

Several senior mendicants discuss the “Questions of Metteyya” from the Pārāyana (Snp 5.3). Each propose an interpretation, and they then ask the Buddha. He praises each reading, but gives his own intended meaning.

A monk asks whether, when the Buddha said that Devadatta was going to hell, he was speaking metaphorically. The Buddha castigates him, wondering how anyone could misunderstand such a straightforward statement, and explains how he reads the minds of others and understands where their deeds will lead them.
A detailed analysis of several central themes, including sense perception, feeling, defilements, kamma, etc.
The Buddha possesses six spiritual powers that enable him to roar his lion’s roar.
Six things lead to non-return.
Six things lead to perfection.
With bad friends, a mendicant cannot fulfill six good qualities, but with good friends they can.
A mendicant who loves to socialize can’t find peace in meditation, but one who loves solitude can.
A god speaks on the factors of non-decline. When the Buddha repeats what he says, Sāriputta gives an explaination.
Without samādhi or immersion it is impossible to realize the higher truths of the path.
Six qualities that enable a mendicant to gain understanding.
Six qualities that enable a mendicant to gain strength in meditation.
Six qualities that enable a mendicant to gain the first absorption.
Six qualities that enable a mendicant to gain the first absorption.
Six qualities that lead to suffering, and whose absence leads to happiness.
Qualities for realizing arahantship.
Qualities for realizing superhuman states.
Qualities leading to happiness.
Qualities for gaining and strengthening good qualities.
Possessing six qualities a mendicant attains to greatness.
Things leading to hell or heaven.
Things leading to hell or heaven.
Things leading to or obstructing the attainment of perfection.
Things leading to growth or deterioration by day or night.
Possessing six qualities a mendicant attains to coolness.
Things that enable or obstruct true understanding while listening to the teachings.
Things that enable or obstruct true understanding while listening to the teachings.
Things that enable or obstruct true understanding while listening to the teachings.
Requirements for becoming a stream-enterer.
Things given up by a stream-enterer.
Things impossible for a stream-enterer.
Things impossible for a stream-enterer.
Things impossible for a stream-enterer.
Things impossible for a stream-enterer.
Things impossible for a stream-enterer.
Things rare to find in the world.
Benefits of stream-entry.
A mendicant on the path cannot see any condition as permanent.
A mendicant on the path cannot see any condition as pleasurable.
A mendicant on the path cannot see any thing as not-self.
A mendicant on the path cannot see extinguishment as suffering.
Reasons for meditating on impermanence.
Reasons for meditating on suffering.
Reasons for meditating on not-self.
Giving up rebirth in the three realms of existence, and developing the three trainings.
Giving up conceit and craving.
Antidotes for greed, hate, and delusion.
Antidotes for bad conduct.
Antidotes for bad thoughts.
Antidotes for harmful perceptions.
Antidotes for harmful properties.
Antidotes for views of gratification, self, and wrong views.
Antidotes for discontent, cruelty, and misconduct.
Antidotes for discontent, unawareness, and desire.
Antidotes for being hard to correct, bad friendship, and distraction.
Antidotes for restlessness, lack of restraint, and heedlessness.
Things that enable or obstruct meditation on the body.
Things that enable or obstruct meditation on various things.
Possessing six qualities, the householder Tapussa sees the Dhamma.
Possessing six qualities, various householders see the Dhamma.
Seven qualities that make a mendicant either agreeable or disagreeable to their fellow monastics.
Seven qualities that make a mendicant either agreeable or disagreeable to their fellow monastics.
The powers of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, samādhi, and wisdom.
The powers of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, mindfulness, samādhi, and wisdom.
The wealth of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom.
The wealth of faith, energy, conscience, prudence, learning, generosity, and wisdom.
Migāra of Rohaṇa is a wealthy man, but even his riches are vulnerable to the vicissitudes of life, unlike spiritual wealth.
The fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
The purpose of the spiritual life is to give up the fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
The fetters of compliance, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, envy, and stinginess.
The underlying tendencies of sensual desire, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
The purpose of the spiritual life is to give up the underlying tendencies of sensual desire, repulsion, views, doubt, conceit, desire to be reborn, and ignorance.
Factors that make a family worth visiting.
Seven persons who are worthy of gifts and veneration.
Seven people found in the world who are like those in water.
Seven persons who are worthy of gifts and veneration.
Seven persons who are worthy of gifts and veneration.
Seven persons who are worthy of gifts and veneration.
Seven persons who are worthy of gifts and veneration.
A mendicant with seven qualities is qualified to graduate.
The Buddha teaches the seven principles of non-decline to the Licchavis at the Sārandada shrine near Vesālī.

King Ajātasattu of Magadha wishes to invade the Vajjians, and sends his minister Vassakāra to tell the Buddha of his intentions. The Buddha recalls the time he taught the seven principles of non-decline to the Vajjians.
The Buddha teaches the seven principles of non-decline to the mendicants.
The Buddha teaches seven principles of non-decline to the mendicants.
The Buddha teaches seven principles of non-decline to the mendicants.
The Buddha teaches seven principles of non-decline to the mendicants.
The Buddha teaches seven principles of non-decline to the mendicants.
Seven qualities leading to the decline of a mendicant who is a trainee.
Seven qualities leading to the decline of a lay follower.
Seven failures for a lay follower.
Seven ruins for a lay follower.
A god speaks of the the seven principles of non-decline to the Buddha, who repeats it to the mendicants.
The Buddha tells of hearing the seven principles of non-decline from a god.
The Buddha tells of hearing the seven principles of non-decline from a god.
The Buddha tells of hearing the seven principles of non-decline from a god. Sāriputta offers additional explanation.
When a friend has seven factors, one should associate with them.
When a mendicant has seven factors, one should stay close to them even if they send you away.
With seven qualities, a mendicant will soon realize the four kinds of textual analysis.
With seven qualities, Sāriputta realized the four kinds of textual analysis.
Seven qualities leading to mastery of meditation.
With seven qualities, Sāriputta became a master of meditation.

Sāriputta visits a sectarian hermitage and hears them saying that someone who lives the spiritual life for twelve years is fit to graduate. The Buddha rejects this, saying that a mendicant is qualified to graduate not merely by completing a certain number of years, but because of their spiritual qualities.

Ānanda visits a sectarian hermitage and hears them saying that someone who lives the spiritual life for twelve years is fit to graduate. The Buddha rejects this, saying that a mendicant is qualified to graduate not merely by completing a certain number of years, but because of their spiritual qualities.
Seven planes of consciousness in which beings may be reborn.
The path factors are the prerequisite for right immersion.
Seven fires, some physical, some psychological.
The brahmin Uggatasarīra has arranged a great sacrifice. On Ānanda’s advice, he asks the Buddha how to make a sacrifice beneficial.
Meditating on seven perceptions leads to the deathless, in brief.
Meditating on seven perceptions leads to the deathless, in detail.
The brahmin Jāṇussoṇī asks the Buddha about behaviors that are a blemish to the celibate life.
By focusing on their own gendered attributes, a man or woman becomes attached to them and develops lust for the opposite sex.
The lay followers of Campā seek a teaching from the Buddha. Together with Sāriputta, they ask the Buddha about what makes a gift fruitful.

Sāriputta and Moggallāna are on tour in the southern hills. A deity informs the laywoman Veḷukaṇṭakī that they are approaching. When Sāriputta expresses his amazement that she speaks with the gods, she goes on to list many other of her own amazing qualities.
The Buddha explains why a noble disciple has no doubt when it comes to the unexplained points.
Seven kinds of rebirth for non-returners, as well as extinguishment by not grasping.
Two gods come to the Buddha on Vulture’s Peak and declare that the nuns are liberated. Moggallāna interrogates one of the deities on what he means, and the Buddha adds further explanation.
The Buddha explains the benefits of giving that are visible in the present life, and one that is only apparent in the next.
Four areas where the Realized One has nothing to hide, and three ways he is irreproachable.
Venerable Kimbila asks the Buddha the reasons for the long-lasting of Buddhism after the Buddha’s demise.
Qualities required for arahantship.
Before his awakening, Moggallāna is struggling with sleepiness in meditation. The Buddha visits him and gives seven ways to dispel drowsiness, and other important teachings.
The incalculable benefits of merit that last for many aeons.
Anāthapiṇḍika’s home was in an uproar due to the badly behaved Sujātā, newly wedded to one of his sons. The Buddha speaks to her on the different kinds of wives.
When someone is angry, they wish ill upon their enemy, and are disappointed if they do well. This sutta includes a powerful set of verses.
A sense of conscience is the foundation that leads step by step to liberation.
A rare discourse on cosmic evolution. The Buddha describes the successive appearance of seven suns and how even the earth will be burned up.

When a king’s fortress is well supplied with seven things and has four kinds of food supplies it is impregnable. Similarly, a noble disciple with seven qualities and the four absorptions is impregnable.
A mendicant who has seven qualities is worthy, with a special emphasis on listening to and learning the teaching.
The blossoming of the Shady Orchid Tree delights the gods of the Thirty-Three, and in the same way, the renunciation of a Buddhist ascetic is delightful.
Sāriputta reflects on what a mendicant should revere in order to develop the wholesome. He reports to the Buddha that these things are indeed found in himself.
Liberation doesn’t happen because you wish for it, but because you develop the path.
It would be better for a monk to embrace a blazing fire than to lie with a maiden.
There have been many great teachers in the past, who should not be reviled. But reviling a single noble disciple is even more serious.
Araka was a famous teacher long ago, when the life span was much greater than today. Nevertheless, he still taught impermanence; how much more is it relevant today!
An expert in monastic conduct has seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct has seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct has seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct has seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct shines with seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct shines with seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct shines with seven qualities.
An expert in monastic conduct shines with seven qualities.
The Buddha teaches the Vinaya expert Upāli a short summary of the teaching.
Seven principles for settling issues and disputes that have arisen in the community.
A mendicant destroys seven defilements.
An ascetic  destroys seven defilements.
A brahmin destroys seven defilements.
A scholar destroys seven defilements.
A bathed initiate destroys seven defilements.
A knowledge master destroys seven defilements.
A noble one destroys seven defilements.
A perfected one destroys seven defilements.
Seven bad qualities starting with faithlessness.
Seven good qualities starting with faith.
Seven people worthy of offerings due to observing impermanence in the eye.
Seven people worthy of offerings due to observing suffering, etc., in the eye, etc.
Develop insight into greed through the awakening factors.
Develop insight into greed through seven perceptions starting with impermanence.
Develop insight into greed through seven perceptions starting with ugliness.
Develop complete understanding, etc., of greed.
Develop complete understanding, etc., of hate, etc.
The eight benefits of practicing the meditation on love.
Eight conditions that lead to the arising of wisdom, its growth and perfection.
Due to eight qualities, a mendicant is displeasing to their fellow monastics, but with the opposite qualities is pleasing.
Due to eight qualities, a mendicant is displeasing to their fellow monastics, but with the opposite qualities is pleasing.
The eight worldly conditions in brief: gain and loss, fame and disgrace, praise and blame, pleasure and pain.
The eight worldly conditions in detail: gain and loss, fame and disgrace, praise and blame, pleasure and pain.
Devadatta’s downfall was the eight worldly conditions.

In a discourse evidently set some time after the Buddha’s passing, Venerable Uttara, staying in a distant land, teaches that a mendicant should review their own failings and those of others. Questioned by Sakka, the Lord of Gods, Uttara affirms that he learned this from the Buddha.
The Buddha praises the grace and restraint of Venerable Nanda.

When a certain monk was admonished, he responded by attacking his reprovers. The Buddha tells the other monks to expel him, explaining that when such a monk lives hidden in the Saṅgha, his corruption can spread to the other monks.

The brahmin Verañja rebukes the Buddha for his lack of respect for senior brahmins. He levels a series of criticisms, each of which the Buddha deflects by redefining terms. The Buddha affirms that his claim to superiority is because he was the first to achieve awakening.

General Sīha, a prominent disciple of the Jains, visits the Buddha against the wishes of Mahāvīra, the Jain leader. He challenges the Buddha by bringing up the various ways that people criticize him. Convinced by the Buddha’s response, Sīha wishes to take refuge in the Buddha; but the Buddha urges him to be cautious and to continue supporting the Jains.
With eight qualities a royal thoroughbred is worthy of a king. Likewise, with eight qualities a mendicant is worthy.
Eight flaws in wild horses, similar to eight flaws in mendicants.
Eight different kinds of stain.
A mendicant with eight qualities is worthy of being sent on a mission.
A woman imprisons a man in eight ways.
A man imprisons a woman in eight ways.
Demons delight in the ocean for eight reasons, and likewise the mendicants delight in the Dhamma for eight reasons.
On a full-moon night, the Buddha was to recite the code of conduct for the monks. However, he remained silent until dawn, due to the presence of a corrupt monk.
The Buddha celebrates the eight amazing qualities of the householder Ugga of Vesālī. Ugga himself speaks of these qualities.
The Buddha celebrates the eight amazing qualities of the householder Ugga of Hatthigāma. Ugga himself speaks of these qualities.

The Buddha celebrates the seven amazing qualities of the householder Hatthaka of Āḷavī. When he hears of this, Hatthaka is anxious to know that no lay people were present. Learning of this, the Buddha adds humility as an eight quality.
The Buddha learns from Hatthaka of Āḷavī how he maintains a large following. He then celebrates Hatthaka’s eight good qualities.
Questioned by his relative Mahānāma, the Buddha explains what makes someone a Buddhist lay follower, a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower practicing for the welfare of all.
Questioned by Jīvaka Komārabhacca, the Buddha explains what makes someone a Buddhist lay follower, a virtuous lay follower, and a lay follower practicing for the welfare of all.
Eight very different kinds of powers.
Eight powers of a perfected one.
Eight lost opportunities for spiritual practice.

Venerable Anuruddha reflects that the teachings is for someone who is of few wishes, content, secluded, energetic, mindful, peaceful, and wise. To these seven thoughts, the Buddha adds an eighth: the teaching is for those who delight in non-proliferation. Practicing in line with these eight thoughts of a great man, Venerable Anuruddha achieves perfection.
Eight ways of giving a gift.
This verse on qualities of a good person appears to be a fragment of text, perhaps originally part of the previous sutta.
Eight grounds for giving.
The rewards of giving are compared to fields of different qualities.
When giving, if you expect a certain result in the next life, you can get it, if the conditions are right.
The good deeds of giving, ethics, and meditation lead to good rebirths, depending on the level to which they are developed.
A good person gives gifts in eight ways.
When a good person is born, it benefits eight kinds of people, like a rain-cloud showering all over the land.
Eight streams of merit, consisting in going for refuge and five kinds of gifts.
Eight kinds of misdeed are conducive to bad outcomes.
When the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. Such a sabbath is glorious.
When the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. The benefits are explained at length.
The Buddha teaches the wealthy laywoman Visākhā that when the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones.

The Buddha teaches the layman Vāseṭṭha that when the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. Vāseṭṭha exclaims that such a practice would be widely beneficial.

The Buddha teaches the laywoman Bojjhā that when the sabbath is observed by following the eight precepts, one lives for that day like the perfected ones. Bojjhā exclaims that such a practice would be widely beneficial.
Some “Agreeable” gods put on a display of music and dance for Venerable Anuruddha, but he is not impressed. He then asks the Buddha how women are reborn in such a heaven.
The Buddha teaches the laywoman Visākhā eight qualities through which a woman may be reborn among the Agreeable gods.
The Buddha teaches the housewife Nakulamātā eight qualities through which a woman may be reborn among the Agreeable gods.
The Buddha teaches the laywoman Visākhā four qualities through which a woman succeeds in this life, and four through which she succeeds in the next.
The Buddha teaches the mendicants four qualities through which a woman succeeds in this life, and four through which she succeeds in the next.
Mahāpajāpatī Gotamī, the Buddha’s foster mother, requests ordination from the Buddha. He declines, until urged to relent by Ānanda. He allows Mahāpajāpatī to go forth on eight conditions.
With eight qualities a monk may be appointed to teach the nuns.
Mahāpajāpatī wishes to go on retreat, so the Buddha teaches her eight principles that summarize the Dhamma in brief.

Dīghajāṇu of the Koliyans asks the Buddha to teach in a way suitable for lay people who enjoy life. The Buddha teaches four practical ways to ensure success in this life, and another four ways to ensure success in the next.
The brahmin Ujjaya is going abroad, and asks the Buddha to teach him. The Buddha teaches four practical ways to ensure success in this life, and another four ways to ensure success in the next.
The Buddha explains how addiction to sensual pleasures is perilous.
With eight qualities, a mendicant is worthy.
With eight qualities, a mendicant is worthy.
Eight kinds of person are worthy.
Eight kinds of person are worthy.
The Buddha describes eight cases where a mendicant has a desire for material things. Sometimes they fall under the sway of that desire, but sometimes they rise above it.
Eight sets of qualities pertain to benefiting oneself and others.
A monk asks for teachings before going on retreat, but the Buddha rebukes him, as he has not practiced sincerely. Nevertheless, he persists, and the Buddha teaches him meditation in detail.
At Gayā, the Buddha describes his meditation before awakening. He was able to see lights and forms, converse with gods, and understand the deeds that made them gods.
Eight dimensions of meditative mastery, based on the vision of certain shapes and colors.
Eight meditative liberations, based on the vision of forms and the attainment of the formless.
Eight kinds of ignoble statement that misrepresent the truth.
Eight kinds of noble statement that convey the truth.
